text: Ten multifloral honey samples produced by *Apis mellifera* L. in the state of Roraima (Brazil) were submitted to physicochemical evaluation and antioxidant activity determination. Thus, the aims of the present study were: (i) to assess the total phenolic content through the Folin-Ciocalteu test and to characterise flavonoids, which were further divided into subclasses including flavones/flavonols and flavonones/dihydroflavonols through the aluminium chloride and 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine colourimetric methods; (ii) to establish the honey color intensity, and (iii) to access their free radical scavenging activity through DPPH test. Total phenolic content, assessed by the Folin-Ciocalteu method, varied between 250 and 548 mg gallic acid kg⁻¹ honey. The analysis of flavonoids content using aluminium chloride, revealed concentrations ranging from 9 up to 48.6 mg quercetin kg⁻¹ honey, whereas using 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine, revealed an important flavonones/dihydroflavonols content ranging from 1805 up to 2606 mg pinocembran kg⁻¹ honey. Honey samples, assessed by DPPH assay, showed antioxidant capacities related to IC₅₀ values ranged from 3.17 up to 8.79 mg/mL, which are extremely low when related to other foods, showing the great antioxidant power of honey. Although flavonones and dihydroflavonols were the most abundant compounds, they did not have a significant correlation with the other analytical parameters evaluated. However, light colour of honey samples did present some correlation with higher levels of total phenolic compounds, flavones/flavonols and consequently greater scavenging activity of the honey samples. Thus, this study gives the first detailed characterisation of Roraima state honey samples that can act as a natural, dietary, source of antioxidant compounds for human consumption.
